tactics.notes: Research cross-checked against: Lottery Post (lotterypost.com charts/threads/vtracs pages), pick34.com (workout library: Original TTT, Pi Rundown 3.14, date sum), pick3edge.com (V-Tracs, 123 Workout/Stacks, pairs charting, sums root chart, lottery terms), pick3world.com (rundown tool, TTT, high/low even/odd filters), lotterypredictor.com (TTT mirror rundown, pyramid rundown worksheet), letsmakebillion.com (VTRAC tutorial, pyramid, sum chart), saliu.com (sums/root-sums math), samlotto.com, lottoexpert.net (wheels), and state lottery play-type rules (SC, PA, FL, LA, NC). IMPORTANT IMPLEMENTATION CONTEXT: (1) Nearly every workout is built on "lottery math" — digit-wise mod-10 addition/subtraction with no carry/borrow; implement once as a primitive: add(a,b) = [(x+y)%10 for x,y in zip(a,b)]. (2) Represent a draw as a tuple of 3 ints (positional/straight) and its "boxed" key as tuple(sorted(draw)); most tracking is done both straight (positional) and boxed. (3) All of these are folk/entertainment systems: every Pick 3 draw is independent and uniform over 000–999, so none of these change the true odds (1/1000 straight, 1/167 six-way box, 1/333 three-way box). They are worth implementing as *descriptive analytics / candidate generators / filters*, which is exactly how players use them: generate a candidate pool (rundown/TTT/VTRAC/followers), then filter it (sum range, high-low, even-odd, key digit, due pair), then choose a play type (straight/box/combo). (4) Useful exact constants verified during research: 1000 straights = 720 no-repeat singles (72%) + 270 doubles (27%) + 10 triples (1%); 220 boxed combos = 120 singles + 90 doubles + 10 triples; sum distribution peaks at 13 and 14 (75 straights each, 7.5%); every sum-last-digit class contains exactly 100 straights; every root sum 1–9 contains exactly 111 straights (root 0 = only 000); each of the 8 high/low patterns and 8 even/odd patterns contains exactly 125 straights, and "mixed" (2:1 or 1:2) patterns cover 750/1000 = 75%; a given digit appears in a draw with p = 1 − 0.9³ = 27.1% (expected gap ≈ 3.7 draws); a specific positional pair has p = 1/100; two given distinct digits co-occur in 54/1000 straights; a specific double pair (aa) appears in 28/1000. (5) The VTRAC space: 5³ = 125 straight vtracs, 35 boxed vtracs, each straight vtrac expands to exactly 2³ = 8 real straights. (6) The 26 "1-off" combos and the 27-cell ±1 cloud are the same object (3³ variants incl. original).

tactics.name: Lottery Math (foundational primitive)
tactics.description: The arithmetic convention underlying almost every Pick 3 workout: addition and subtraction are performed digit-by-digit modulo 10, with NO carrying or borrowing. E.g., 914 + 123 = 037 (9+1=0, 1+2=3, 4+3=7); 803 = 914 - 111. Every rundown, TTT fill, mirror, date sum and pyramid uses this.
tactics.how_to_compute: def lmath_add(a, b): return tuple((x + y) % 10 for x, y in zip(a, b)); def lmath_sub(a, b): return tuple((x - y) % 10 for x, y in zip(a, b)). Draws are 3-tuples of ints 0-9. Also define lottery_sum(n) for scalars used in date sums: repeatedly take n % 10 (players 'keep only the last digit') — e.g., 8+22=30 -> 0, or 1+18=19 -> 9.
tactics.popularity: Universal — implicit in every workout posted on Lottery Post and pick34.com

tactics.name: Play types: Straight / Box / Straight-Box / Combo / Pairs / 1-Off
tactics.description: How Pick 3 bets are settled. STRAIGHT: match all 3 digits in exact order (1/1000, typical payout 500:1 per $1). BOX: match in any order — a 6-way box is 3 distinct digits (6 permutations, 1/166.67, ~$160 per $1), a 3-way box is a double like 112 (3 permutations, 1/333.33, ~$330 per $1). STRAIGHT/BOX: a $1 play split as 50c straight + 50c box on the same number — pays box amount on any-order match plus straight amount if exact. COMBO: buys every permutation of your digits as separate straight bets (cost 6x for singles, 3x for doubles, 1x for triples) so any order pays full straight. PAIRS: FRONT PAIR = first two digits in exact order, SPLIT PAIR = 1st and 3rd digits, BACK PAIR = last two digits (each 1/100, ~$50 per $1). 1-OFF: wins when each drawn digit is within +/-1 (with 9->0 wrap) of your digit; there are 26 qualifying non-exact combinations plus the exact match.
tactics.how_to_compute: permutations: perms = set(itertools.permutations(number)) — len is 6 (all distinct), 3 (double), 1 (triple). classify(n): if len(set(n))==3 -> 'single/6-way'; elif len(set(n))==2 -> 'double/3-way'; else 'triple'. box_match(pick, draw): sorted(pick)==sorted(draw). straight_match: pick==draw. front_pair: pick[0:2]==draw[0:2]; split_pair: pick[0]==draw[0] and pick[2]==draw[2]; back_pair: pick[1:3]==draw[1:3]. one_off_cloud(n) = {((n[0]+i)%10,(n[1]+j)%10,(n[2]+k)%10) for i,j,k in itertools.product((-1,0,1),repeat=3)} — 27 combos including exact; 1-OFF wins on any of the 26 non-exact members (payout varies by how many digits are off). combo_cost(n) = len(set(itertools.permutations(n))) x straight price.
tactics.popularity: Universal — official game mechanics in every US state

tactics.name: Mirror numbers (+5) and mirror workouts
tactics.description: Each digit's 'mirror' (a.k.a. sister) is the digit 5 away: 0<->5, 1<->6, 2<->7, 3<->8, 4<->9. Players believe drawn digits often 'come back as their mirror'. The full mirror of a draw (mirror all 3 digits, e.g. 123 -> 678) is called its sister number. Mirror workouts generate all partial-mirror variants of the last draw (mirror one digit, two digits, or all three), and mirrors are also applied to date sums, rundown rows, and TTT grids. A related folk transform is the 6/9 'flip' (some players also flip 4/7 on printed slips): replace 6s with 9s and vice versa.
tactics.how_to_compute: mirror(d) = (d + 5) % 10. sister(draw) = tuple(mirror(d) for d in draw). Full mirror set: variants = {tuple(draw[i] if not m[i] else mirror(draw[i]) for i in range(3)) for m in itertools.product((0,1), repeat=3)} — 8 combos including the original (2^3 masks). Flip variant: flip(d) = {6:9, 9:6}.get(d, d) applied per digit. Players typically play the sister number straight/box and add 1-digit-mirror variants to a candidate pool.
tactics.popularity: Very high — one of the most-cited concepts on Lottery Post; embedded in VTRAC and most TTT workouts

tactics.name: VTRAC system ('trac')
tactics.description: Variable Tracking: maps the 10 digits into 5 vtrac digits, pairing each digit with its mirror. V1={0,5}, V2={1,6}, V3={2,7}, V4={3,8}, V5={4,9}. A draw converts to a 3-digit vtrac (written v###): 586 -> v142. This halves the tracking space: there are only 125 straight vtracs (35 boxed 'vtrac families'), and each straight vtrac expands back to exactly 8 real straight numbers (2 choices per position). Players track vtracs instead of raw numbers because patterns repeat faster: they look for repeating vtracs (same vtrac hitting again within a few draws), due vtracs per position, due vtrac sums (range 3-15), and 'double vtracs' (a vtrac with a repeated vtrac digit, e.g. v112) — a double vtrac covers both real doubles (e.g. 11x, 66x) AND mirror pairs (e.g. 16x, 61x), so 'double vtrac watch' is how players anticipate doubles/mirror-pair draws.
tactics.how_to_compute: to_vtrac(d) = (d % 5) + 1 per digit; vtrac(draw) = tuple(to_vtrac(d) for d in draw). expand(vt) = itertools.product(*[( (v-1), (v-1)+5 ) for v in vt]) -> 8 straight numbers (digit choices for vtrac v are v-1 and v+4). Boxed vtrac family key = tuple(sorted(vtrac(draw))) — 35 families; a family expands to the union of boxed expansions. vtrac_sum = sum of the 3 vtrac digits (3..15); track its frequency/skips like regular sums. Double vtrac detection: len(set(vtrac(draw))) == 2; triple vtrac: len==1. Tracking algorithm: convert draw history to vtrac history; per position keep counts over a window (e.g. 30 draws) and current skip per vtrac digit 1-5; flag 'due' vtracs (highest skip) and 'hot' vtracs (highest count); play 2-3 vtracs per position, expand the positional sets back to real digits (each vtrac -> its 2 digits) and take the cartesian product as the candidate pool. Also track 'vtrac return': probability-weighted rule that last draw's vtrac (or one of its box arrangements) repeats — check next draw's vtrac against previous.
tactics.popularity: Very high — has a dedicated Lottery Post section (lotterypost.com/vtracs) and dozens of long-running state threads

tactics.name: Tic-Tac-Toe (TTT) workouts
tactics.description: Family of workouts that place draw digits into a 3x3 grid, fill the rest by a rule, then 'read out' candidate numbers along rows, columns, diagonals and shape patterns. Classic single-draw version: put the last draw down the main diagonal, fill remaining cells by counting up (+1 lottery math) from the last digit starting at the bottom-right region and sweeping around; a -1 (count-down) grid is built the same way. Mirror TTT (as taught by Lottery Predictor): build a left grid from the last THREE draws stacked as rows (newest on top), then build a right grid where every cell is the mirror (+5) of the left cell; scan the right grid for playable combos, especially diagonals and top-to-bottom columns. Date-sum TTT variants seed one row/diagonal with the draw and another with the date sum digits.
tactics.how_to_compute: Grid g[r][c], r,c in {0,1,2}. VARIANT A (+1 diagonal): g[0][0],g[1][1],g[2][2] = d1,d2,d3 (last draw). Fill the 6 empty cells with d3+1, d3+2, ... d3+6 (each %10) in a fixed traversal — the documented convention starts at the bottom-right area and sweeps left then around: order (2,1),(2,0),(1,0),(0,1),(0,2),(1,2). (Traversal order is a convention knob; implementations differ — expose it as a parameter.) VARIANT B (-1): same but subtract. VARIANT C (mirror TTT): left grid rows = last 3 draws, newest first: g[0]=draw_t, g[1]=draw_(t-1), g[2]=draw_(t-2); right grid m[r][c] = (g[r][c]+5)%10. VARIANT D: stack grid = variant C left grid alone. READOUT (applies to any grid): lines = 3 rows + 3 columns + 2 diagonals = 8 base combos; extended patterns players also read: 4 corner-triples {(0,0),(0,2),(2,0)},{(0,0),(0,2),(2,2)},{(0,0),(2,0),(2,2)},{(0,2),(2,0),(2,2)}; 'cross' = center with any 2 of the 4 edge midpoints; 'diamond' = edge midpoints {(0,1),(1,0),(2,1)},{(0,1),(1,2),(2,1)}. Dedupe boxed (sorted-tuple set) to get the final candidate pool (typically ~10-20 boxed combos per workout).
tactics.popularity: Very high — the most common YouTube/blog Pick 3 workout; many state-specific TTT threads on Lottery Post

tactics.name: 111 rundown (and 369 variant)
tactics.description: The simplest rundown: repeatedly add 111 to the last draw using lottery math. Because each digit just cycles +1, the rundown returns to the start after 10 steps, producing a 10-row column ladder. Players play rows from the middle of the rundown, rows containing the date sum or its mirror, or rows sharing digits with recent hits. The 369 rundown is identical mechanics with step 369; 222/555 etc. also appear.
tactics.how_to_compute: rows = [draw]; for i in range(9): rows.append(lmath_add(rows[-1], (1,1,1))). Yields 10 rows (all 10 phase-shifts of each digit). For 369: step=(3,6,9) — note gcd considerations: since each per-digit step is coprime-or-not with 10 independently, the row cycle length is lcm of 10/gcd(step_i,10); for 111 and 317 it is 10. Selection heuristics to implement: (a) return rows k=3..6, (b) rows where date-sum digit or its mirror appears, (c) rows whose sum falls in 8-19.
tactics.popularity: High — canonical beginner rundown, built into every rundown generator tool

tactics.name: 123 rundown and the '123 workout' stack (+123 / -111 / +235)
tactics.description: Two related classics. (1) 123 rundown: repeatedly add 123 (lottery math) to the last draw, listing rows until it cycles. (2) The 'old timers' 123 workout' (a.k.a. Stacks, per pick3edge): from the last draw compute exactly three derived numbers — draw+123, draw-111, draw+235 (all lottery math) — stack them as a 3x3 grid, and read combinations out of the stack TTT-style (across, down, diagonals, diamond, corners, cross, H pattern), deduping to ~10 boxed plays. Worked example from draw 914: +123 -> 037, -111 -> 803, +235 -> 149; stack [[0,3,7],[8,0,3],[1,4,9]] yields plays like 037, 803, 149, 081, 304, 739, 089, 341, 039, 743.
tactics.how_to_compute: Rundown: rows=[draw]; for i in range(9): rows.append(lmath_add(rows[-1],(1,2,3))). Stack workout: r1=lmath_add(draw,(1,2,3)); r2=lmath_sub(draw,(1,1,1)); r3=lmath_add(draw,(2,3,5)); grid=[r1,r2,r3]; then apply the TTT readout function (rows, cols, diagonals, corners, diamond, cross) and dedupe boxed. Note the no-carry rule: 9+1=0, 0-1=9.
tactics.popularity: High — 'one of the oldest Pick 3 systems'; the stack version is the pick3edge signature workout

tactics.name: 317 rundown
tactics.description: Popular rundown using the constant 317: repeatedly add 317 to the last draw with lottery math, producing rows until the sequence cycles back (10 rows). Sold as books and built into apps; players commonly play the first 3-7 rows below the seed, rows containing key/date-sum digits, or intersect the rundown with a second workout (e.g., mirrors) and play the overlaps.
tactics.how_to_compute: rows=[draw]; for i in range(9): rows.append(lmath_add(rows[-1],(3,1,7))). Full cycle = 10 rows (each per-digit step 3,1,7 is coprime with 10). Variants to support: subtract-317 rundown (lmath_sub), and 'both directions' (compute +317 and -317 ladders and look for combos appearing in both). Selection: same filter heuristics as the 111 rundown; a common play is rows 1-5 boxed.
tactics.popularity: High — frequently requested on Lottery Post; commercial books/spreadsheets exist for it

tactics.name: +1/-1 rundowns (one-up/one-down) and the 1-off cloud
tactics.description: Perturbation workouts: from the last draw (or a predicted number), generate every variant where each digit is moved up or down by 1 (with 9->0 wrap). The full cloud is 3^3=27 combos (including the original) — exactly what the official '1-OFF' bet covers (26 non-exact). Simpler versions just play draw+111 and draw-111, or move one position at a time (+100, -100, +010, -010, +001, -001). Players use this on the theory that 'the machine was one click away'.
tactics.how_to_compute: cloud(n) = {tuple((n[i]+delta[i])%10 for i in range(3)) for delta in itertools.product((-1,0,1),repeat=3)}. one_position_variants(n) = {lmath_add(n,e) for e in [(1,0,0),(9,0,0),(0,1,0),(0,9,0),(0,0,1),(0,0,9)]} (adding 9 == subtracting 1 mod 10). Typical play: box the 27-cloud of a strongly-predicted number, or buy the official 1-OFF play type instead.
tactics.popularity: Medium-high — the logic behind the official 1-OFF play type and many 'one up one down' threads

tactics.name: Date sum workout
tactics.description: Uses the calendar date as a key-digit generator. Date sum = lottery sum of month + day (e.g., 7/21 -> 7+21=28 -> 8; 1/18 -> 19 -> 9). Players compute the date sum and its mirror (+5) each day and use them as 'highlighted' digits: they circle those digits wherever they appear in rundowns/TTT/pyramids and prefer candidate combos that contain or 'touch' them. Extensions: include the year digits, or use month-digit-sum + day-digit-sum.
tactics.how_to_compute: date_sum(m, d) = (m + d) % 10 (players phrase it as 'keep the last digit'; some instead sum all digits then reduce — support both: variant2 = digital-style: s = sum(int(c) for c in f'{m:02d}{d:02d}'); ds = s % 10). key_digits = {ds, (ds+5)%10}. Filter: keep candidate combos c where set(c) & key_digits. As a standalone generator: play all boxed combos containing both key digits (54 straights / ~13 boxed for distinct digits).
tactics.popularity: High — standard companion filter for rundowns and the daily pyramid

tactics.name: Pyramid / dateline pyramid workout
tactics.description: A date-based triangle rebuilt each day, 'good for all states'. The bottom row is the date's digits (MM DD YY, sometimes plus the last draw's digits); each cell above is the lottery-math sum of the two cells below it, forming a pyramid. The date-sum digit and its mirror are highlighted, and players hunt for 3-digit combos formed by adjacent/touching cells that include a highlighted digit. Lottery Predictor distributes printable pyramid rundown worksheets; Lets Make Billion publishes the all-states pyramid daily and tracks how many states it 'hit'.
tactics.how_to_compute: base = [m1,m2,d1,d2,y1,y2] digits of the date (variant: base += last draw digits). pyramid = [base]; while len(pyramid[-1])>1: prev=pyramid[-1]; pyramid.append([(prev[i]+prev[i+1])%10 for i in range(len(prev)-1)]). Highlight h = date_sum and (date_sum+5)%10. Candidate extraction: all triples of mutually adjacent cells — horizontally adjacent triples within a row (r[i],r[i+1],r[i+2]) and 'triangle' groups (row[i], row[i+1], row_above[i]); keep those whose digit set intersects the highlight set; dedupe boxed. (Adjacency definition varies by author — implement it as a pluggable pattern list.)
tactics.popularity: Medium-high — daily-published workout with dedicated followings

tactics.name: Hot / cold / overdue (due) digits with skip tracking
tactics.description: The core statistical workout. Over a rolling window of past draws (commonly 10, 20, 30, or 50), count how often each digit 0-9 appears (overall and per position). Hot digits = most frequent; cold = least frequent; due/overdue = digits with the longest current 'skip' (consecutive draws since last appearance). Since a digit appears in a draw with p = 1 - 0.9^3 = 27.1% (expected gap ~3.7 draws), players flag a digit as due when its skip is roughly 2x+ the expected gap (skip >= 7-8). Positional skip tracking does the same per position (p=0.1, expected gap 10). Players combine: play combos mixing 1-2 hot digits with 1 due digit, or key on the most-out digit.
tactics.how_to_compute: history = list of draws, newest last. freq[d] = sum(1 for draw in history[-W:] for x in draw if x==d) (or presence-count: draws containing d). skip[d] = number of draws since d last appeared: iterate from newest backwards until d in draw. positional: freq_pos[p][d] and skip_pos[p][d] using draw[p]==d. Rankings: hot = top-3 by freq; cold = bottom-3; due = top-k by skip. Due threshold: skip[d] > 2 * (1/0.271) ≈ 7.4 for any-position; skip_pos > 20 for positional. Same machinery generalizes to any trackable object (pairs, sums, vtracs, patterns): maintain dict object->last_hit_index and object->window_count; due score = skip / expected_gap where expected_gap = 1/p - 1 (p = object's theoretical probability per draw).
tactics.popularity: Universal — every lottery site publishes hot/cold charts; skip ('out') tracking is standard Lottery Post vocabulary

tactics.name: Sums (sum totals) filter
tactics.description: The sum of the 3 digits ranges 0-27 with a symmetric triangular-ish distribution: sums 13 and 14 are most common (75 straight combos each, 7.5%), and the middle band dominates — sums 10-17 cover 560/1000 (56%) and sums 8-19 cover 760/1000 (76%). Players filter candidate pools to a sum window (classically 10-17 or 8-19), track which sums are due (Lottery Post and lottoreport.com publish 'date each sum last hit' charts), and some states offer a 'Sum It Up' bet paying on the sum itself.
tactics.how_to_compute: s(draw) = sum(draw). Exact distribution: count[s] = len([1 for a,b,c in itertools.product(range(10),repeat=3) if a+b+c==s]) -> {0:1,1:3,2:6,3:10,4:15,5:21,6:28,7:36,8:45,9:55,10:63,11:69,12:73,13:75,14:75, then mirrored down to 27:1} (count[s]==count[27-s]). Filter: keep candidates with lo <= s(c) <= hi (default 8..19). Due-sum tracking: skip per sum value using the generic skip tracker; expected gap for sum s = 1000/count[s] draws (straight).
tactics.popularity: Very high — sums charts are among the most-viewed Lottery Post charts

tactics.name: Root sums
tactics.description: The digital root of the combo: add the digits, then keep adding the result's digits until one digit remains (e.g., 589 -> 22 -> 4). Root sums run 1-9 (root 0 exists only for 000), and each root 1-9 covers exactly 111 straight combos (11.1%) — a perfectly balanced 9-bucket partition, which is why players love tracking 'due root sums'. A common workout: identify the root sum absent longest, then play boxed combos from that root-sum family intersected with other filters (key digit, sum range).
tactics.how_to_compute: root(draw): s=sum(draw); return 0 if s==0 else 1+(s-1)%9 (digital root formula). Family generation: family[r] = [c for c in all_1000 if root(c)==r]; boxed family = {tuple(sorted(c))}. Track skips per root 1-9 (expected gap ≈ 9 draws; due when skip > ~18). Note root r aggregates sums {r, r+9, r+18} (e.g. root 1 = sums 1,10,19 -> 3+63+45=111 straights).
tactics.popularity: High — dedicated Lottery Post chart; staple of prediction threads

tactics.name: Sum last digit (SLD)
tactics.description: The last digit of the sum (e.g., 589 -> sum 22 -> SLD 2). SLD runs 0-9 and — a fact players often miss — each SLD class contains exactly 100 straight combos (perfectly uniform 10%), because SLD d aggregates sums {d, d+10, d+20} whose counts always total 100. Players track due SLDs exactly like root sums and use SLD as a coarser 10-bucket filter; it also connects to VTRAC math (mirror pairs share relationships through +5 shifts).
tactics.how_to_compute: sld(draw) = sum(draw) % 10. family[d] = [c for c in all_1000 if sld(c)==d] (always 100 combos). Skip tracking with expected gap = 10 draws; flag due when skip > ~20. Use as filter: keep candidates whose SLD is in the chosen due/hot set.
tactics.popularity: Medium — official Lottery Post chart category, used by sum-system players

tactics.name: Pairs analysis (front / split / back pairs, due pairs)
tactics.description: Every draw ABC contains three positional pairs: front AB, split A_C, back BC. Players maintain a 10x10 (00-99) hit chart for each pair position, plus an 'any-arrangement' chart of the 55 unordered digit-pairs, recording times drawn and draws since last hit. Hot pairs recur, and 'due pairs' (longest out) get played either via the official pair bet types or by attaching a third digit (often a key digit, hot digit, or the pair's mirror digit) to form full combos. Specific positional pair p = 1/100 per draw; two given distinct digits co-occur (any positions) in 54/1000 draws; a specific double-pair aa appears in 28/1000.
tactics.how_to_compute: For each draw (a,b,c): front=(a,b), split=(a,c), back=(b,c). Maintain three dicts pos_pair_stats[kind][(x,y)] = {'hits': n, 'skip': draws_since_last}. Unordered pairs: for each of the 3 combinations itertools.combinations(sorted(draw),2), update unordered_stats[pair]. Due pair = max skip relative to expected gap (100 draws positional; 1000/54≈18.5 draws for unordered distinct; 1000/28≈35.7 for doubles). Combo generation from a due pair (x,y): candidates = {tuple(sorted((x,y,z))) for z in range(10)} (10 boxed combos), optionally ordered per position for straight play; prioritize z in {key digit, mirror(x), mirror(y), hot digits}.
tactics.popularity: Very high — Lottery Post has a dedicated pairs system page; several states sell pair bets directly

tactics.name: Key digit strategy
tactics.description: Pick 1-2 'key' digits believed near-certain to appear in the next draw, then restrict all plays to combos containing them. Key digits are sourced from: the date sum and its mirror, the most-overdue digit, the hottest digit, followers of the last draw's digits, or the digit that has hit most in the last 5 draws. One key digit cuts the straight space from 1000 to 271 (boxed: 220 -> 55 combos containing digit d); two key digits cut it to 54 straights (distinct d1,d2).
tactics.how_to_compute: contains(c, d) = d in c. Pool with 1 key: [c for c in all_1000 if k in c] (271 straights; boxed: 55 of 220). Pool with 2 keys: [c for c in all_1000 if k1 in c and k2 in c] (54 straights if k1!=k2). Key selection heuristics to implement as strategies: (a) k = argmax(skip[d]); (b) k = date_sum or mirror(date_sum); (c) k = top follower of last draw's most recent digit (see follower matrix); (d) k = hottest digit in last W draws. Combine with sum/high-low filters to shrink further.
tactics.popularity: High — 'key digit' is core Lottery Post vocabulary; most prediction posts name key digits

tactics.name: Doubles tracking (when doubles are 'due')
tactics.description: Doubles (exactly two matching digits, e.g. backslash 112) make up 270 of 1000 straights, so they should hit ~27% of draws — about 3 in 10, one every ~3.7 draws on average. Players track the skip since the last double and start playing doubles when the skip stretches past ~7-10 draws ('doubles are due'). They then choose WHICH double via: due double-pairs (the 90 doubles' aa-pair skip chart), mirror-pair triggers, and double-vtrac triggers (a double vtrac draw like v122 that came back as a mirror pair, e.g. 1-6-x, is read as a signal a true double is coming). Sub-patterns tracked: 'front doubles' (AAb), 'back doubles' (aBB), 'split doubles' (AbA).
tactics.how_to_compute: is_double(c) = len(set(c))==2. skip_since_double = draws since last is_double. Expected gap = 1/0.27 ≈ 3.7; flag 'doubles due' when skip >= ceil(2*3.7)≈8. Double selection: for each digit d, track skip of double-pair dd (a draw containing >=2 d's; p=28/1000, expected gap ~36); play the top-k most-out dd pairs, attaching third digits z by heuristic (mirror(d), hot digits, date sum): combos = {tuple(sorted((d,d,z)))}. Positional split: classify prior doubles into AAb/AbA/aBB by positions of the repeated digit and track which arrangement is due for straight play.
tactics.popularity: High — 'doubles are due' is a standing topic; dedicated commercial systems exist (Double Trap)

tactics.name: Triples tracking
tactics.description: Triples (000-999 repeats, e.g. 555) are 10 of 1000 straights — 1% of draws, expected once per 100 draws. The community strategy: track draws since the last triple and begin playing all 10 triples (or a shortlist by digit-due) once ~90+ draws have passed without one. Triple 'triggers' players watch: a run of doubles, 'false triples' (a double plus the mirror of the doubled digit, e.g. 550 -> 000/555 watch), and triple vtracs (v111 etc. = 8 real combos incl. two true triples).
tactics.how_to_compute: is_triple(c) = len(set(c))==1. skip_since_triple = draws since last. Trigger: skip >= 90 -> play all 10 triples straight (cost $10/day at $1) until hit or budget stop. false_triple(c) = is_double(c) and (the odd digit == mirror(doubled digit)) — e.g., (5,5,0): doubled=5, odd=0=mirror(5) -> True; on a false triple, shortlist triples {doubled digit, mirror digit}. Digit choice ranking: triple d where skip of digit d as a triple (draws since ddd) is longest.
tactics.popularity: Medium — periodic frenzies when a state goes long without a triple

tactics.name: Series / consecutive numbers
tactics.description: 'Series' combos contain three consecutive digits in any order, with 0-9 wrap: boxed series are 012,123,234,345,456,567,678,789,890,901 — 10 boxed combos = 60 straights (6% of draws). Players track when a series last hit and play the due series set; extensions include 'broken series' / skip-counting sets (e.g. 024, 135 — digits 2 apart) and 'mirror-consecutive-pair' triplets (a consecutive pair plus a mirror digit).
tactics.how_to_compute: series_boxed = {tuple(sorted(((s)%10,(s+1)%10,(s+2)%10))) for s in range(10)} (10 sets). is_series(c) = tuple(sorted(c)) in series_boxed. Skip tracking: expected gap = 1000/60 ≈ 16.7 draws; due when skip > ~34. Broken series: {tuple(sorted((s%10,(s+2)%10,(s+4)%10))) for s in range(10)}. Consecutive-pair detection: any two digits of c differ by 1 mod 10 — use for 'MCP' style filters (consecutive pair + mirror of one of them as third digit).
tactics.popularity: Medium — commonly listed pattern category in charts and prediction posts

tactics.name: High/low and even/odd pattern filters
tactics.description: Digits split Low = 0-4, High = 5-9 and Even/Odd. Each draw maps to one of 8 positional H/L patterns (LLL...HHH) and 8 E/O patterns; every single pattern covers exactly 125 straights (12.5%). Players filter to 'balanced' patterns: the six 2:1/1:2 mixes cover 750/1000 = 75% of draws, so all-low/all-high (or all-even/all-odd) candidates get cut. Combined filtering (H/L x E/O) plus the '10-draw rule' — when a pattern group hasn't appeared in 10+ draws it's considered due — drives pattern-timing plays.
tactics.how_to_compute: hl(c) = tuple('H' if d>=5 else 'L' for d in c); eo(c) = tuple('E' if d%2==0 else 'O' for d in c). Boxed pattern = sorted counts, e.g. hl_boxed = (num_high, num_low) in {(0,3),(1,2),(2,1),(3,0)} with straight counts 125,375,375,125. Filters: keep candidates where 1 <= num_high <= 2 (and/or 1 <= num_even <= 2). Pattern skip tracking: generic skip tracker keyed by hl_boxed/eo_boxed (expected gaps: 8 draws for 0:3 or 3:0, 2.7 draws for mixes); due when skip > 2x expected (the community's 10-draw rule for the rarer groups).
tactics.popularity: High — standard filters in every Pick 3 software package; saliu.com and pick3world both formalize them

tactics.name: Digit distribution / positional frequency charts
tactics.description: Baseline charting: how often each digit 0-9 has hit in each position (P1/P2/P3) and overall, versus the 10% expectation, over a chosen window and over all history. Used to spot positional biases (real lotteries with ball machines occasionally show wear biases), select positional pools for wheels, and feed hot/cold rankings. Also includes 'longest out straight/boxed combo' tracking — which of the 1000 straights (or 220 boxed) has gone longest without hitting.
tactics.how_to_compute: counts = [[0]*10 for _ in range(3)]; for draw in history[-W:]: for p,d in enumerate(draw): counts[p][d]+=1. Deviation score per (p,d): (counts[p][d] - W/10) / sqrt(W*0.1*0.9) (z-score). Longest-out tracking: last_seen = {}; for i,draw in enumerate(history): last_seen[draw]=i; last_seen_boxed[tuple(sorted(draw))]=i; out = sorted(all_combos, key=lambda c: last_seen.get(c,-1))[:k]. Chi-square over counts flags machine bias (the only statistically defensible edge-hunt in this whole catalog).
tactics.popularity: Universal — every stats site publishes these charts

tactics.name: Follower theory and hit-and-follow charts
tactics.description: The belief that specific digits/numbers historically 'follow' others into the next draw. Digit-level folk rules circulate (after 1 look for 7 or 4; after 9 look for 3; the 'Old Man's Zero' rule: a drawn 0 is accompanied/followed by a 3 or 6 about 90% of the time, and when it isn't, another 0 is imminent). Number-level follower systems (e.g., AANewYork's books, Lucky's Followers) map each boxed combo to a list of combos that most often appeared in the draw after it historically; hit-and-follow charts are the tabulated form. Implementable as empirical transition tables built from a state's own history.
tactics.how_to_compute: Digit follower matrix: M = 10x10 zeros; for t in range(len(history)-1): for i in set(history[t]): for j in set(history[t+1]): M[i][j]+=1. Row-normalize -> P(j appears next | i appeared). Top followers of digit i = argsort desc of M[i]. Number-level: F = defaultdict(Counter); for t: F[tuple(sorted(history[t]))][tuple(sorted(history[t+1]))]+=1; followers(combo) = F[boxed].most_common(k). With only ~2 draws/day the number-level table is sparse — smooth by backing off to digit-level products or pair-level followers (Counter keyed by (pair_t, pair_{t+1})). Old Man's Zero as a rule: if 0 in last draw and not ({3,6} & set(last draw)): key digits for next = {0,3,6}.
tactics.popularity: High — long-running Lottery Post threads and multiple commercial follower books

tactics.name: Repeat / carryover digit rule
tactics.description: Rule of thumb that at least one digit from the previous draw returns in the next draw. Mathematically true most of the time by chance alone: if the previous draw had 3 distinct digits, P(no repeat) = 0.9^3-based ≈ 0.7^3 = 34.3%, so ≥1 digit carries over ~65.7% of the time (more when tracked boxed over midday+evening). Players build candidate pools that include exactly one or two digits from the last draw, and track the empirical carryover rate/skips for their state.
tactics.how_to_compute: carry(prev, cand) = len(set(prev) & set(cand)) (0-3). Pool: [c for c in all_1000 if carry(last_draw, c) >= 1] (~657 straights for a 3-distinct-digit previous draw — so combine with other filters), or the tighter 'exactly one carryover' pool. Tracking: per draw compute carry(history[t], history[t+1]); maintain skip since carry==0 (a 'no-repeat' event) and play no-repeat pools when no-repeat is 'due', or vice versa.
tactics.popularity: Medium-high — commonly stated as 'one digit always returns' in workout videos

tactics.name: Pi system (3.14 rundowns)
tactics.description: Novelty rundowns keyed to pi. Two circulating forms: (1) Pi Rundown 3.14 (pick34.com): treat 314 as the step and repeatedly add it to the last draw with lottery math, exactly like a 317/123 rundown; (2) multiplication form: multiply the previous draw (as an integer) by 3.14 (some divide by 3 after), and read 3-digit windows from the resulting digit string as candidates. Related constants used the same way: 317, 369, 618 (phi). Lottery Post threads also discuss x/pi - c variants and combining the pi row with a 999 complement (subtract the number from 999, then run down the result).
tactics.how_to_compute: Form 1: rows=[draw]; for i in range(9): rows.append(lmath_add(rows[-1],(3,1,4))). Form 2: n = 100*d1+10*d2+d3; s = str(int(round(n*3.14))) (variant: int(n*3.14/3)); candidates = {tuple(int(x) for x in s[i:i+3]) for i in range(len(s)-2)}; pad with leading zeros. 999 complement primitive (used in several systems): comp(draw) = tuple((9-d) for d in draw) — no mod needed; then run any rundown on comp(draw).
tactics.popularity: Low-medium — recurring novelty on Lottery Post and pick34.com

tactics.name: Stack systems (draw stacking)
tactics.description: Instead of transforming one draw, stack the last N draws (typically 3-5, mixing midday/evening) as rows of a grid and mine it for candidates: vertical columns, diagonals in both directions, 'V' and 'X' shapes, and vertical/diagonal pairs which are then completed with key digits. The mirror-TTT left grid is the N=3 special case. Some players stack a draw with its mirror and its 999-complement instead of older draws.
tactics.how_to_compute: grid = [history[-1], history[-2], ..., history[-N]] (newest first), an Nx3 matrix. For N=3 apply the standard TTT readout. For general N: candidates from every vertical triple (grid[r][c],grid[r+1][c],grid[r+2][c]), every down-right and down-left diagonal triple, and pair mining: collect all vertical/diagonal pairs, feed to the pairs-completion routine (attach key/hot/mirror third digit). Alternative stack: grid = [draw, sister(draw), comp(draw)] then TTT readout.
tactics.popularity: Medium — 'stacks' videos are a YouTube staple; formalized on pick3edge

tactics.name: Wheeling Pick 3 digits
tactics.description: Coverage systems: choose a pool of N digits you believe contains the next draw's digits, then generate all (or a guaranteed subset of) 3-digit combinations from the pool. A full 6-digit box wheel = C(6,3) = 20 single combos with a '3 if 3' guarantee (if all three winning digits are in your 6, you have the boxed winner); 7 digits -> 35; 8 -> 56. Abbreviated wheels trade combos for weaker guarantees. Positional wheels assign a digit set to each position and take the Cartesian product for straight play. Key-digit wheels lock one digit into every line.
tactics.how_to_compute: Full box wheel (singles): combos = [tuple(sorted(t)) for t in itertools.combinations(pool, 3)]. Include doubles: itertools.combinations_with_replacement(pool, 3) (C(N+2,3) combos). Positional/straight wheel: combos = list(itertools.product(P1, P2, P3)) — cost |P1|*|P2|*|P3|. Key wheel: [tuple(sorted((k,)+t)) for t in itertools.combinations_with_replacement(pool, 2)]. Abbreviated wheels: implement as covering designs — greedy set-cover over the target guarantee (e.g., every 3-subset of pool is box-covered by >=1 ticket) if you need fewer lines than the full wheel. Payout-coverage check: a wheel 'guarantees' a box hit iff winning boxed key ⊆ pool.
tactics.popularity: High — wheel generators on Lottery Post, lottoexpert.net, steveplayer.com and most apps

thread.fetch_method: Blocked by sitewide Cloudflare managed challenge. WebFetch returns HTTP 403; curl with full Chrome UA + Accept headers also gets 403 with 'cf-mitigated: challenge' header and a 'Just a moment...' Turnstile/JS challenge page (server: cloudflare, challenges.cloudflare.com CSP). Applies to every tested URL: thread pages /1 and /6, base thread URL, homepage, /rss endpoints, and the mobile mirror w.lotterypost.com. Not a login wall — pure bot mitigation requiring JS execution. No bypass was attempted per instructions. Fallbacks all empty: Wayback Machine CDX has ZERO snapshots of thread/360017*; Google cache is dead (deprecated); archive.today returned 429 rate-limit; Google/Bing/DuckDuckGo have not indexed thread 360017 at all (though they index many other LotteryPost threads).
thread.thread_title: UNKNOWN — could not be verified from any accessible source (site challenge-gated, thread unindexed by search engines, no web archives exist)
thread.total_pages_estimate: At least 6 (the /6 URL implies page 6 exists); actual total unverifiable. For scale, comparable long-running NY threads grow huge (NY Dream Team 2024 reached 1,800+ pages).
thread.notes: POLLING VERDICT: an automated system CANNOT poll lotterypost.com with plain HTTP clients (requests/urllib/curl/WebFetch) — the entire domain including RSS feeds sits behind a Cloudflare managed challenge that requires JavaScript execution and likely Turnstile solving. A custom User-Agent will not help (full browser UA already gets challenged). Options that remain are manual browser access or negotiating access with the site owner; headless-browser challenge-solving would constitute bot-protection bypass and was not attempted. THREAD DATING (inferred, not confirmed): neighboring thread IDs anchor the timeline — 343848='NY Dream Team 2023', 348317='NY Dream Team 2024', 353356≈Jan 30 2025, 357331='NY Dream Team' (active June 2026, likely the 2026 edition). At LotteryPost's ~4,500 new threads/year, thread 360017 was likely created around Q2 2026, consistent with an actively running New York Pick 3 discussion at page 6+ as of July 2026. CONTENT: systems_mentioned and recent_predictions are empty because no post content was accessible anywhere — nothing could be verified, and generic knowledge of what NY Pick 3 threads discuss (rundowns, mirrors, TTT, pairs) would be speculation about THIS thread. Search-result snippets from sibling NY Dream Team threads only revealed community norms (drama-free predictions sharing) and one methodology fragment (pair-follows-pair analysis, zeros in state database) — from thread 326147/348317 era, NOT from 360017.

datasets.name: Lottery Daily Numbers/Win-4 Winning Numbers: Beginning 1980 (OFFICIAL Socrata dataset — NY State Gaming Commission; covers BOTH Numbers midday+evening AND Win-4)
datasets.api_endpoint: https://data.ny.gov/resource/hsys-3def.json (also .csv; SODA API — supports $select/$where/$order/$limit; free app token via X-App-Token header lifts anonymous throttling)
datasets.fields: One row per calendar date: draw_date (calendar_date), midday_daily (text, the Pick-3 midday number), midday_daily_sum (number), evening_daily (text), evening_daily_sum (number), midday_win_4, midday_win_4_sum, evening_win_4, evening_win_4_sum, midday_daily_booster, evening_daily_booster, midday_win_4_booster, evening_win_4_booster. CAUTION: daily values are text and usually keep leading zeros ('044', '061') but at least one truncated value exists ('0' on 2024-12-22 evening) — always normalize with zfill(3).
datasets.update_freshness: Posted once daily at ~5:00-5:01 AM ET with the PREVIOUS day's midday+evening results (verified: rowsUpdatedAt 2026-07-16 09:01:11 UTC = 5:01 AM ET, and at 00:41 ET on 7/17 the newest row was 7/15 — the 7/16 results already live on nylottery.ny.gov were not yet in Socrata). Effective lag: ~6.5h after the evening draw, ~14.5h after the midday draw. NOT suitable for right-after-draw pulls; perfect for history.

datasets.name: NY Lottery official-site live JSON API (undocumented backend of nylottery.ny.gov — 'nyl-api')
datasets.api_endpoint: https://nylottery.ny.gov/nyl-api/games/numbers/draws (Numbers only); https://nylottery.ny.gov/nyl-api/games/all/draws (every draw game); backup mirror: https://nylottery.ny.gov/drupal-api/api/all/draw_games_fallback?_format=json. MUST send a User-Agent header (Cloudflare blocks bare urllib/curl defaults — matches your existing 'Vizlow/1.0' gotcha).
datasets.fields: JSON: data.draws[] with drawTime (epoch ms, midnight ET of draw date), drawPeriod (1=midday, 2=evening), drawNumber (sequential, e.g. 23223), gameName ('numbers'), gameId ('9'), status (4=pending, 20/22=resulted), resultDate (epoch ms when result generated), results[0].primary = array of digit strings in drawn order (e.g. ["6","0","0"] — leading zeros preserved). Pending draw also has wagerCloseTime — ideal for scheduling your poll. Returns last 10 Numbers draws (~5 days) + the next pending draw.
datasets.update_freshness: Effectively real-time: result generated seconds after entry close (observed resultDate = 14:15:15 ET midday / 22:20:15 ET evening) and served here around the public reveal (~2:30 PM / ~10:30 PM ET). Verified live: 7/16 evening result '600' present the same night while Socrata still lacked it. Undocumented API — could change without notice, so pair with a fallback.

datasets.name: LotteryUSA — New York Numbers (reputable aggregator, scrapeable)
datasets.api_endpoint: No public JSON API — but pages are fully server-rendered HTML (verified: curl with a browser User-Agent returns the last ~10 draws in a c-results-table with date + individual ball digits, e.g. Jul 16, 2026 → 6 0 0). Easy to parse with BeautifulSoup.
datasets.fields: Draw date, three drawn digits, top prize; ~10 most recent draws per page plus year archives.
datasets.update_freshness: Fast — footer showed 'Updated: Thu, Jul 16, 2026 @ 11:00 PM' for the 10:30 PM draw (~30 min lag). Good near-real-time fallback.

datasets.name: Lottery.net — New York Numbers (aggregator, scrapeable)
datasets.api_endpoint: No API; server-rendered HTML (verified via curl — shows results and 'Today at 2:30pm' next-draw banner). Year archives at /new-york/numbers-midday/numbers/<year> and /new-york/numbers-evening/numbers/<year> for backfill.
datasets.fields: Draw date, midday/evening winning digits, payout references.
datasets.update_freshness: Updates shortly after each draw (minutes-scale, not contractual). Second scraping fallback.

datasets.name: 'pick 3 and 4 daily' (wyni-sezg) — AVOID: community-created derived view of hsys-3def, not maintained by the Gaming Commission
datasets.api_endpoint: https://data.ny.gov/resource/wyni-sezg.json (same lag as parent dataset; unofficial view that could be deleted by its creator)
datasets.fields: Subset/rearrangement of the official hsys-3def columns.
datasets.update_freshness: Inherits the parent dataset's once-daily ~5 AM ET refresh.

data.draw_times: NY Numbers is drawn twice daily, 7 days/week. Official site game config (pulled from nylottery.ny.gov's own page-data): draw_time = "Midday (2:30 PM) Evening (10:30 PM)" ET, close_time (last ticket sale) = "2:15 PM & 10:20 PM" ET. Since the Sept 2024 switch to computerized digital drawings, the RNG result is actually generated seconds after entry close — the official API's resultDate stamps are 2:15:15 PM (midday) and 10:20:15 PM (evening) ET — but it is publicly revealed/posted at ~2:30 PM and ~10:30 PM ET. Posting timeline observed on 2026-07-16/17: official nyl-api JSON has the result by the ~2:30/~10:30 reveal; LotteryUSA updated by 11:00 PM (~30 min); the data.ny.gov Socrata dataset gets both of the day's draws the NEXT morning at ~5:00-5:01 AM ET.
data.payouts: Official table from NY Lottery's "Numbers Odds & Prizes" PDF (edit.nylottery.ny.gov/sites/default/files/2020-03/Numbers_Odds_and_Prizes.pdf), consistent with 9 NYCRR 5009.2. STRAIGHT (exact order): odds 1 in 1,000; $0.50 play → $250; $1.00 → $500. BOX 3-WAY (2 identical digits, any order): odds 1 in 333; $0.50 → $80; $1.00 → $160. BOX 6-WAY (3 unique digits, any order): odds 1 in 167; $0.50 → $40; $1.00 → $80. STRAIGHT/BOX 3-WAY (sold as a $1 play = 50c straight + 50c box): odds 1 in 333; exact-order match pays $330 (straight+box), any-other-order match pays $80 (box only). STRAIGHT/BOX 6-WAY ($1 play): odds 1 in 167; exact order $290, any other order $40. COMBINATION 3-WAY (all 3 straight permutations on one ticket): costs $1.50 → wins $250, or $3.00 → $500; odds 1 in 333. COMBINATION 6-WAY (all 6 permutations): costs $3.00 → $250, or $6.00 → $500; odds 1 in 167. FRONT PAIR (first 2 digits in order): odds 1 in 100; $0.50 → $25; $1.00 → $50. BACK PAIR (last 2 digits in order): odds 1 in 100; $0.50 → $25; $1.00 → $50. Wagers run 50c to $5 in 50c multiples; prizes scale linearly with stake. Prizes are FIXED amounts (not pari-mutuel), though regulation 9 NYCRR 5009 lets the commission cap liability / switch to a pari-mutuel pool (up to 50% of sales) — in practice heavily-played numbers get sales cutoffs rather than reduced prizes. Note true odds vs payout: straight house edge = 50% (1,000-to-1 event paying 500-to-1); every bet type carries the same ~50% takeout except straight/box exact ($330 on a $1 play beats the $250+$80 split components trivially — still ~50% EV overall).
data.fifty_draw_source: Hybrid, two GET requests, no auth needed. (1) History from the official Socrata SODA API — one call returns 50 calendar days = 100 draws (both midday+evening per row): curl -H "User-Agent: Vizlow/1.0" "https://data.ny.gov/resource/hsys-3def.json?$select=draw_date,midday_daily,evening_daily&$order=draw_date%20DESC&$limit=50" (use $limit=25 if you want exactly 50 individual draws). (2) Top up the 1-2 most recent days Socrata hasn't posted yet (anything after ~5 AM ET yesterday) from the official site's live API: curl -H "User-Agent: Vizlow/1.0" "https://nylottery.ny.gov/nyl-api/games/numbers/draws" — map drawPeriod 1→midday / 2→evening, join digits from results[0].primary, dedupe against the Socrata rows by date+period. Normalize every number with str.zfill(3) (Socrata has at least one truncated leading-zero value). For ongoing right-after-draw automation: poll the nyl-api endpoint starting 2:30 PM / 10:30 PM ET (the pending draw's wagerCloseTime field tells you the next cutoff), retry every 1-2 min until status flips from 4 to 20/22, with LotteryUSA HTML as scrape fallback and Socrata as the next-morning reconciliation source. Optionally register a free Socrata app token (X-App-Token header) to avoid anonymous throttling.
data.notes: All endpoints verified live on 2026-07-17 ~00:40 ET. Key evidence: Socrata metadata rowsUpdatedAt = 2026-07-16T09:01:11Z (5:01 AM ET daily refresh, data through 7/15 only), while https://nylottery.ny.gov/nyl-api/games/numbers/draws already contained the 7/16 evening result (6-0-0, drawNumber 23223, resultDate 22:20:15 ET) — proving nyl-api is the only near-real-time official source. The nyl-api base was extracted from nylottery.ny.gov's Gatsby bundle (GATSBY_API_DRIVEN_URL = "/nyl-api"); it is undocumented, so treat it as best-effort and keep the LotteryUSA scrape fallback. Cloudflare fronts nylottery.ny.gov — always send a User-Agent header. The nyl-api per-game feed only holds ~10 draws (5 days), so 50-draw history must come from Socrata. Draw-time provenance: the official site's own game config JSON (page-data for /draw-game) states Midday 2:30 PM / Evening 10:30 PM with 2:15 PM & 10:20 PM sales cutoffs. Payout provenance: official NY Lottery PDF (Numbers_Odds_and_Prizes.pdf on edit.nylottery.ny.gov) cross-checked against 9 NYCRR 5009.2 (law.cornell.edu). Ignore third-party 'average payout' figures (e.g. $721 straight) seen on nylottery.org — that site is unofficial and those are statistics, not the prize schedule. Also relevant: the dataset added booster columns (midday_daily_booster etc.) for the newer Booster add-on; sums columns (midday_daily_sum) are handy if you ever add Lucky Sum logic. Scratch files from this session live in the session scratchpad (numbers_draws.json, numbers_odds.pdf, dg.json) if the parent wants raw evidence.

feature_ideas: Honest prediction ledger (highest priority): every prediction is locked and timestamped BEFORE the draw, then auto-graded after results come in against real payout tables (straight ~$500/$1, box $80-$160/$1). Show cumulative net P&L, ROI, win rate, max losing streak, and peak-to-trough drawdown. This combines Lottery Post's 'prize ratio' standardization with DrawAnalytics' backtest metrics — no site does this well for a personal dashboard.

Random-baseline benchmark on every stat: plot each strategy's hit rate and P&L against the expected value of random picks (straight = 0.1% per draw per combo, box 6-way = 0.6%) with a binomial-test p-value. The LucianBuddy GitHub ensemble does this (1.0% vs 0.5% baseline) and it's the single most honest evaluation device seen anywhere — commercial tools all omit it.

Per-strategy scoreboard: track each prediction method (hot-digits, due-pairs, Markov followers, ensemble, gut picks) as a separate 'account' with its own hit rate, prize ratio, and P&L curve; rank them like Lottery Post's Top Predictors leaderboard, and enforce a minimum-sample gate (Lottery Post requires 500 picks before you appear on the board) before displaying a strategy's stats as meaningful.

Backtest engine with walk-forward simulation: run any strategy or filter stack over the full draw history computing spend, winnings, net, ROI, drawdown, and longest cold streak (DrawAnalytics model). Use walk-forward evaluation (fit on draws 1..N, predict N+1) to kill look-ahead bias, and label backtested results distinctly from live pre-registered results.

Auto-update pipeline: cron-scraped midday/evening state draw results that grade pending predictions, refresh every chart, and push a notification within minutes of each draw — DrawAnalytics' 'updated after every draw' freshness is a big differentiator over stale hobby sites.

Positional digit frequency + skip charts: per-position (hundreds/tens/units) digit hit frequency over selectable windows (30/60/100/365 draws), plus skip charts showing draws-since-last-hit for each digit with median-skip and max-skip markers (Saliu's median-skip strategy; Lotto Pro's skip-and-hit sequence chart). Include an honest 'due is not predictive' caveat toggle.
Pairs grid: 10x10 heatmaps for front/split/back pairs showing frequency and current skip, plus an overdue-pairs list (LetsMakeBillion's most-used tools are exactly this).

VTRAC layer: mirror-digit abstraction (0/5=v1, 1/6=v2, ...) collapsing 1,000 straights to 243 vtrac combos — vtrac frequency, vtrac sums/root sums, vtrac pairs, and vtrac triggers/followers charts (LetsMakeBillion and Lottery Post communities treat vtracs as core, not exotic).

Combination filter workbench (Deflate 3 / Inspector 3 clone): start from 1,000 straight or 220 box combos and stack filters — sum range, root sum, odd/even count, high/low, contains/excludes digit, vtrac, skip thresholds — with a live readout of combos remaining, cost to play all, coverage %, and expected value, plus a per-filter elimination report (SamLotto's 'filter report' transparency is what makes its 68 filters usable).

Triggers & followers / Markov panel: for the last draw, show which digits, pairs, and combos historically followed it (1st-3rd order transitions with Bayesian shrinkage so rare states don't produce fake 100% signals) — LetsMakeBillion's 'Number Triggers & Followers' is popular; the shrinkage idea comes from the LucianBuddy repo.

Sums & structure dashboard: sum distribution (0-27) vs theoretical curve, root sums, boxed-structure tracking (singles/doubles/triples ratio vs expected 72/27/1%), consecutive-digit and all-odd/all-even pattern tracking (Pick34.com's tracking charts, Inspector 3 classifications).

Ensemble predictor with rank fusion: several scoring models (decay-weighted frequency, Markov chain, skip regression, Monte Carlo consensus, small NN) each score all 1,000 combos by full enumeration, fused via Borda ranking into a top-N candidate list with confidence scores — the LucianBuddy architecture, which is the most sophisticated open-source design found.

Simulated bankroll mode: choose bet type (straight/box/combo), stake, and strategy; simulate the bankroll trajectory going forward with Monte Carlo confidence bands so streaks and ruin probability are visible before real money is involved (extends DrawAnalytics' max-consecutive-losses metric).
Missing/longest-out straight combinations tracker: list straights and boxes that haven't hit in X years with their theoretical expected gap (LetsMakeBillion 'Missing Straight Combinations').

Hot/cold with configurable windows and decay weighting, split by midday vs evening and day-of-week (LotteryPredictor's state hot/cold pages are its whole traffic engine; seasonality splits come from the ML writeups' feature engineering).

House-edge honesty widget: for any play list, display exact true odds, expected loss per dollar (~50% house edge), and per-draw EV — the anti-Pick3Sniper feature; that product's dishonest 'coverage' framing (showing 6 digits removed when only 4 are eliminated) is the cautionary tale.

Rundown/workout toys with tracked performance: tic-tac-toe grids, +123/-111 rundowns, mirror workouts (Pick34.com collections, Lottery Post systems forum staples) — fun to include, but every generated pick flows into the honest ledger so the dashboard proves whether they work.
One-click win checker & data export: paste any tickets to check against history for straight/box/pair wins (SamLotto), plus CSV/JSON export of draws, predictions, and grades for outside analysis.
priorArt.notes: Research method: web survey (July 2026) of commercial pick-3 software, community sites, backtesting platforms, open-source repos, and ML writeups. Lotterypost.com blocks direct fetches (403) so its details come from search snippets of its predictions board, Deflate 3, Inspector 3, and Top Predictors pages. Key cross-cutting insights: (1) The genre's biggest credibility gap is honest self-evaluation — almost every commercial tool (Pick3Sniper, Lotto Pro) overclaims, while the two honest mechanisms found are Lottery Post's standardized-prize 'prize ratio' leaderboard with minimum-pick thresholds, and DrawAnalytics' backtester that reports net P&L, ROI, drawdown, and max losing streak with explicit warnings about cherry-picking and overfitting. A personal dashboard that pre-registers picks and grades them against real payout tables with a random baseline would beat everything surveyed. (2) The standard analysis vocabulary users expect: positional digit frequency, hot/cold, skip charts (with median skip), pairs grids (front/split/back), vtrac (mirror-digit) charts, sums/root sums, boxed structure (single/double/triple), triggers-and-followers, overdue lists, and missing-straight trackers — LetsMakeBillion is the most complete free reference implementation of these. (3) Filtering workflow: Deflate 3 (reduce 1000/220 combos via stacked filters, rank survivors) + Inspector 3 (classify any combo list by odd/even, high/low, sums, pairs) + SamLotto's per-filter elimination reports define the expected UX; showing cost/coverage/EV of the surviving play list is the honest upgrade. (4) ML: no writeup demonstrates a real edge (pick 3 draws are i.i.d.), but the LucianBuddy/lottery-pick3-skills repo has the best architecture ideas — 8-model Borda-rank fusion, full 1000-combo enumeration, Bayesian-shrunk Markov transitions, 200-period backtests vs random baseline, and an information-theoretic ceiling estimate (~0.45 bits) — i.e., treat ML as one ranked scorer among many and report against baseline. (5) Payout math for the P&L simulator: straight pays ~$500/$1, 6-way box ~$80, 3-way box ~$160; expected return ~50% — the dashboard should surface this house edge rather than hide it.

sites.name: Lottery Post — Prediction Board + Top Predictors
sites.features: Community prediction board: members post picks before each drawing; system auto-matches against results and stores full history. Standardized prize amounts per game type make cross-state comparison fair; per-member stats pages filterable by state/game; 'hit ratio' and 'prize ratio' (winnings vs cost) are the headline metrics; Top Predictors leaderboard requires 500+ picks in 30 days and recent activity — an anti-cherry-picking gate. The gold standard for honest accuracy tracking.

sites.name: Lottery Post — Deflate 3 / Inspector 3 / Systems Forum
sites.features: Deflate 3: reduces the 1,000 straight or 220 box combos via stacked filters and ranks survivors by how often they appear in the filtered list. Inspector 3: classifies any combo list by odd/even, high/low, sums, root sums, pairs to reveal bias. Plus combinations analysis, wheels, and a systems forum full of member workouts (rundowns, tic-tac-toe, first-position indicators, skip/hit spreadsheets).

sites.name: DrawAnalytics
sites.features: Modern (46-state) auto-updating dashboard with frequency charts, long-gap numbers, pattern tools, combo generator. Standout: pick 3/pick 4 backtester — input up to 10 combos, bet type (straight/box), wager, 1-5yr range; outputs total spend, wins, winnings, net P&L, ROI, win rate, max consecutive losses, and drawdown. Their blog explicitly preaches honest backtesting: pre-commit combos, avoid cherry-picking/overfitting, judge net P&L not gross winnings.

sites.name: LetsMakeBillion — Pick 3 Tools
sites.features: The most complete free pick-3 chart suite: hot/cold numbers and digits, overdue pairs and pair predictions, number triggers & followers, VTRAC triggers & followers, positional/box frequency, VTRAC frequency (sum, root sum, sum last digit), pair frequency over up to 2 years, most-frequent numbers & pairs, overdue (skip-based) numbers & pairs, missing straight combinations, and even/odd-high/low structural attributes — all per state.

sites.name: LotteryPredictor.com
sites.features: State-by-state hot/cold numbers for pick 3/pick 4 split by midday/evening draw, daily prediction lists, 'Number Vault' premium picks, results checking, lucky numbers by date. Feature set is thin analytically, but its per-state per-drawtime page structure and daily refresh are why it dominates search traffic.

sites.name: LottoStrategies.com
sites.features: Digit-wise hit frequency analysis for pick 3/pick 4 by state with configurable draw windows; 'My Lotto Analysis' runs 21 algorithms covering hot/cold, odd/even trends, and digit pattern analysis.

sites.name: SamLotto SamP3P4
sites.features: 80+ statistical charts; 68 filters (50 basic + 18 advanced) with 1-click default parameters, fault tolerance, and per-filter reports showing exactly how many tickets each filter removed; skip analysis; past-results combination search; multiple prediction algorithms with verification against actual results and per-method prediction history; wheel generator; straight/box/pair win checking.

sites.name: magayo Pick
sites.features: Analyzes each digit position to recommend a digit pool and filters, then full-wheels the selected digits into tickets; stats on every number, positional digit, permutation, and digit sum; one-click draw-result updates; free tier + $39 pro. Good example of a guided pick-digits-then-wheel workflow.

sites.name: Pick3Sniper
sites.features: Web-based 'Artificial Intuition Unit' showing 10 cluster panels with digits removed; user picks 3 of remaining 6 digits. Widely criticized (LottoExposed, LottoAnalyst) for claiming 100% accuracy and for misleading reduction math (displays 6 digits eliminated when only 4 are). Its value to this project is as an anti-pattern: never obscure true coverage/odds.

sites.name: Pick34.com
sites.features: Visual due lists (root sums), boxed filter charts (sums, cut sums), sums-and-roots tracking charts for last 25 draws, history charts per game worldwide, tic-tac-toe rundown collections, pick-3 calculators, and a paid 'special users group' of systems. Old-school but shows what daily-game players actually track.

sites.name: Saliu skip systems / Lotto Pro skip charts
sites.features: Skip-based strategy tooling: skip = draws between hits of a digit/number; play when current skip <= median skip; charts distinguish 'actual dueness vs math dueness'; Lotto Pro adds skip-and-hit-sequence charts. Source of the median-skip concept worth building as a chart overlay.

sites.name: GitHub: LucianBuddy/lottery-pick3-skills
sites.features: Best open-source architecture found: 8 heterogeneous models (positional-frequency composite, 3rd-order Markov with Bayesian shrinkage, cross-period pattern features, 1000x1000 transition matrix, Monte Carlo consensus, mean-reversion binomial tests, K-Medoids coverage optimization, numpy NN ensemble) fused via Borda ranking over full enumeration of 1,000 straight / 90 double / 120 single candidates. Reports 200-period backtests against explicit random baselines (1.0% vs 0.5% straight) and an information-theoretic prediction ceiling (~0.45 bits) — a template for honest ML claims.

sites.name: GitHub: other open-source analyzers (Lofea, LottoPipeline, Ahmad-Alam/Lottery-Prediction)
sites.features: Lofea: lottery feature-engineering library for ML (group-membership targets rather than exact numbers). Callam7/LottoPipeline: modular pipeline with frequency/decay analysis, clustering, Monte Carlo, Markov, Shannon entropy, deep learning. Ahmad-Alam: 4-layer LSTM with rolling-window sequences. Plus byte-pushers/pick3-lottery-web-scraper for results scraping. Collectively useful for pipeline structure, decay-weighted frequency, and scraper patterns.

sites.name: ML lottery-prediction writeups (Medium ARIMA/LSTM, GameSeer, ReelMind)
sites.features: Feature-engineering catalog: positional digit frequency, pair co-occurrence, digit sums, even/odd counts, consecutive-digit flags, skip/lag features, day-of-week and midday/evening seasonality; models tried: ARIMA, LSTM/Transformer, Random Forest, XGBoost, Monte Carlo. Unanimous conclusion: no demonstrated edge over random — reinforcing that the dashboard's value is tracking and honesty, not oracle claims.
